Operational Viewer Proof Build DiscoveryCase Study

What Cal proves

Clarity before dispatch.

Cal demonstrates a reusable AtlasFlow pattern for field service: a messy request becomes AI triage, a structured job record, dashboard visibility, human review, and a clear recommended next action.

01

Who is this for?

Owners, dispatchers, office managers, and small service teams who need to triage inbound work without missing urgent issues.

02

Product Thesis

Service requests arrive messy and inconsistent, forcing dispatchers to interpret urgency, risk, and next steps manually. Cal can turn that unstructured input into clear, prioritized, dispatch-ready work while directing owner attention to the requests that matter most.

03

Validation

Does providing dispatchers an AI-generated category, urgency, risk signal, confidence indicator, summary, dispatch notes, and recommended next action help them triage requests faster and more consistently—while helping owners identify urgent or high-risk jobs sooner?

AtlasFlow OV Template

Cal Operational Viewer

Service request triage + dispatch clarity. An AtlasFlow Operational Viewer is a reusable decision surface that shows how messy operational input becomes structured context, risk or priority signals, and clear next actions.

Cal — Operational Viewer

Service request triage + dispatch clarity

Customer-facing proof layer for Cal’s operating logic: what came in, what changed, what Cal understood, what needs attention, and what should happen next.

Static OV · MVP proof

04What needs attention?

05What should happen next?

Dispatch, review, schedule, or brief

Selected Detail PanelTrust layer

Mrs. Thompson — Emergency Leak

Original input

Water is leaking from the ceiling below an upstairs bathroom and continues after an attempted toilet shutoff.

AI interpretation

Cal identifies the category as Leak / Water Damage, marks the urgency as Emergency, and flags active water damage.

Risk Reason

The leak continues after an attempted shutoff, indicating active property risk and the need for immediate human review.

Recommended Next Action

Dispatch a same-day emergency plumber immediately and call the customer before arrival.

Status

Triaged · Owner Attention

Human decision point

The dispatcher or owner reviews Cal’s recommendation and remains responsible for technician assignment, timing, and customer communication.

Workflow Replay

Cal: Form → Table → AI → Automation → Views

Form→Table→AI→Automation→Views
Proof Artifact

The Cal Operational Viewer is backed by the working Airtable proof: raw service records enriched with AI-generated job type, priority, RYG confidence, summary, and recommended next action.

Static OV

Polished proof layer for portfolio, stakeholder review, early demo, and product positioning.

Dynamic OV path

Next version can connect one live section to Airtable or another operating data source.

OV principle

Decision cockpit, not dashboard dump: attention, risk, reason, and next action.

Working proof

Working MVP evidence.

The proof artifact shows the service request record after Cal has written practical triage fields back into the operating record for dispatcher and owner review.

  • Job category and urgency classification
  • Customer risk and Red / Yellow / Green confidence
  • Issue summary, dispatch notes, and recommended next action
  • Operational views for emergencies, review, dispatch, and the Morning Brief

Build architecture

Lean stack. Clear operating signal.

The Cal MVP keeps the stack intentionally lean: Airtable stores service requests and brief records, Zapier runs the workflow, OpenAI performs triage and brief generation, and AppSheet or a simple dashboard presents the results for human review.

01

Form

Captures customer, phone, address, and a raw service request from a call, text, email, form, or handwritten note.

→
02

Table

Stores the raw request, structured triage outputs, workflow status, and Morning Brief-ready fields.

→
03

AI

Generates category, urgency, issue summary, customer risk, recommended action, dispatch notes, RYG confidence, and owner-attention status.

→
04

Automation

Maps and writes the structured AI output back into the original service request record.

→
05

Views

Organizes work into Emergency / High Priority, Needs Review, Ready for Dispatch, Owner Attention, and Morning Brief views.

Customer discovery

Validated against a real plumbing operator.

Doug Isley, an owner-operator and technology-forward advisor, helped ground Cal in the reality of a plumbing business already using a mature field-service platform.

Current environment

ServiceTitan + multiple add-ons

Isley Plumbing has used ServiceTitan since 2018 and spends approximately $60,000 annually. Doug’s view was that it covers most core operating needs, with customer communication already about 60% automated.

Strongest workflow reaction

Technician Note Cleaner

Doug responded most strongly to turning rough field notes into a clean office summary with parts needed, unresolved issues, and the next action. The likely daily user would be the office manager.

Design implication

Keep the MVP simple and compelling

The discovery did not reveal a broad platform gap to replace ServiceTitan. It reinforced Cal’s role as a focused proof of reusable AI workflow design—not an attempt to become an all-in-one trades platform.

What the discovery changed

Cal remains a service-request triage MVP because it clearly demonstrates messy input becoming structured action. The next discovery-led extension should test technician-note cleanup and office handoff rather than expanding into scheduling, routing, inventory, or payments.

Business value

Speed and clarity for small service teams.

Cal reduces the mental triage load on dispatchers and gives owners a cleaner read on what deserves attention. It is not trying to be a full dispatch platform; it proves the workflow that turns operational noise into clear decisions.

Morning Brief sample

Today’s owner focus

Start with active water damage and emergency drain risk. Move emergency and high-priority jobs before routine work, then review Red and Yellow confidence items before assigning technicians.

2High-risk jobs
3Needs review
5Ready to dispatch
